name: Isabelle Augenstein
bio_arc: Isabelle Augenstein is a tenure-track associate professor at the University of Copenhagen, Department of Computer Science, affiliated with the CoAStAL NLP group. Previously a postdoctoral research associate at UCL and PhD from the University of Sheffield. Her research centers on statistical NLP and machine learning, with focus areas in weakly supervised and low-resource learning applied to information extraction, machine reading, and fact-checking. She has organized shared tasks and workshops on automated fact-checking at major venues.

canonical_works.title: Learning to Read for Automated Fact Checking
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Keynote and research program articulating the vision of using NLP for automated fact-checking, framing document encoding as central to verification.

canonical_works.title: MultiFC: A Dataset for Multi-domain Fact Checking
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Established multi-domain fact-checking benchmark that remains widely used for evaluating claim verification systems.

canonical_works.title: Generating Fact Checking Explanations (with Atanasova)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Pioneered generating natural language explanations for fact-checking verdicts, advancing explainability in verification.

canonical_works.title: A Diagnostic Study of Explainability Techniques for Text Classification (with Atanasova)
canonical_works.why_it_matters: Diagnosed faithfulness vs plausibility in explanations, showing that plausible-sounding rationales may not faithfully reflect model reasoning.

signature_ideas: Weak supervision and low-resource learning can dramatically reduce annotation costs for fact-checking tasks
Fact-checking should produce explanations, not just verdicts — explainability is essential for trust
Multi-domain evaluation is critical; systems that work on political claims may fail on scientific claims
Document-level encoding is more informative than sentence-level for evidence retrieval

core_questions: Does the system produce explanations alongside verdicts?
How does the system handle claims in domains it wasn't trained on?
Is the evidence retrieval robust to varied document structures?
Can the system operate with limited labeled data (low-resource settings)?
decision_rules: Prefer systems that generate explanations over black-box verdicts
Favor multi-domain approaches over single-domain training
Prioritize evidence quality over claim complexity
Weight recall over precision when missing evidence is more costly than false positives
failure_taxonomy: Domain shift: system trained on political claims applied to scientific claims
Evidence drift: supporting documents change over time, invalidating cached evidence
Annotation bias: label distributions skewed by annotator political leanings
Explanation hallucination: generated rationales that don't align with actual evidence
preferred_abstractions: Stance detection as a decomposition of verification

preferred_evidence: controlled benchmark results across multiple domains
ablation studies showing contribution of each component
error analysis with domain-specific failure cases
critique_style: Asks whether verification outputs are explainable and auditable
Probes generalization across claim types and domains
Checks whether low-resource scenarios were evaluated
Looks for evidence that generated explanations faithfully reflect source documents
blind_spots: May over-prioritize academic benchmark performance over practical deployment constraints
Focus on NLP methods may underweight search/retrieval infrastructure quality
European academic context may miss real-time production concerns

known_debates: Whether fact-checking should be fully automated or human-in-the-loop
Tension between speed and accuracy in real-time verification
Whether multi-domain training helps or hurts domain-specific performance
anti_patterns: Ignoring temporal drift in evidence documents
Treating stance detection as simple classification rather than structured reasoning

question_playbook.situation: Evaluating a claim verification system's evidence retrieval
question_playbook.question: What happens when the top evidence documents disagree with each other?

question_playbook.situation: Reviewing an evidence report's confidence calibration
question_playbook.question: Does the confidence score actually correlate with verification accuracy across domains?
Does each evidence citation in the report actually support the claim it is attributed to?
Can the system break a compound claim into verifiable sub-claims?

domain_relevance.summary: Augenstein's deep expertise in automated fact-checking, multi-domain verification, and explainability makes her an ideal lens for evaluating evidence quality, claim classification, and verification pipeline design.
best_used_for: Evaluating claim verification and stance detection logic
Assessing evidence report explainability and citation quality
Reviewing multi-domain generalization of search-and-verify systems

quote_bank.quote: How can natural language processing methods fill this gap and help to automatically check facts?
Weak supervision can dramatically reduce annotation costs for fact-checking, making it feasible to train verification systems with limited labeled data.

source_confidence.rationale: Multiple Tier A sources (institutional profile, Google Scholar) plus Tier B keynote covering research vision. Core expertise well-documented.

freshness.notes: Augenstein is currently active at UCPH; profile reflects ongoing research direction.

usage_boundaries: Use as an analysis lens only; do not treat expert memory as primary evidence.
Do not imitate private personality or fabricate beliefs.
